items.title: Strategic Company Evaluation
items.details_html:
ASSIGNMENT

2–3 page applied analysis evaluating how a pharmaceutical company or comparable stakeholder navigates a key strategic challenge related to innovation, access, pricing, or commercialization. Students may select a case discussed in class or a comparable real-world example. The assignment should identify the core challenge, analyze key tradeoffs, and present a clear recommendation. 

RUBRIC

Criteria Points
Strategic challenge: Clearly identifies a relevant challenge related to innovation, access, pricing, or commercialization, and explains why it matters. 4
Company or stakeholder response: Describes how the pharmaceutical company or comparable stakeholder navigated the challenge, including key decisions, constraints, or pressures. 4
Strategic analysis: Goes beyond summary to analyze the tradeoffs, risks, stakeholder tensions, and consequences of the choices made. 4
Evaluation and judgment: Offers a clear point of view on the effectiveness of the response, including what worked, what did not, and/or what could have been done differently. 4
Use of evidence and writing quality: Uses relevant evidence from class materials, readings, discussion, or real-world context. Writing is clear, organized, concise, and appropriate for a 2–3 page applied analysis. 4

SUBMISSION GUIDELINES

  • 12-point Times New Roman or similar readable font
  • Double spacing
  • 1-inch margins
  • Page numbers
  • A clear title
  • In-text citations or footnotes 
  • A short reference list at the end (does not count toward page limit)
  • Naming convention: LastName_FirstName_StrategicCompanyEvaluation

NOTE: Lecture notes and class slides should not be used as cited sources. If you want to reference an idea discussed in class, cite the assigned case, reading, article, report, or other original source that supports the point.

items.title: APEx: Executive Summary (Final)
items.details_html:
Details: 

Students must submit an Executive Summary (Final) + Peer Review (of your Executive Summary Draft) of their APEx. The objective of the Executive Summary is to provide an overview of the APEx organization and projects, and to describe and analyze the challenges faced and proposed solutions. Executive Summaries are compiled into an anthology and distributed in advance of APEx Forum, in order to prepare participants and facilitators for breakout group discussions. The summary will also serve as the foundation for students’ APEx Forum slide presentations.

Executive Summary Drafts will go through a Peer Review prior to being submitted to the department. Your assigned Peer Reviewer can be found here. The department’s expectation is that peer reviewers will meet to discuss reviewer comments in the rubric. Your peer is expected to return their Peer Review Rubric to you via email no later than 11:59pm Sunday, June 28. You are expected to review and incorporate their notes into your Executive Summary (Final) and upload the Executive Summary (Final) + their Peer Review (of your Executive Summary Draft) documents by 11:59pm Wednesday, July 1.

 

Assignment Instructions:

Executive Summary Instructions here

Executive Summary (Final) Samples: One, Two

Peer Review Rubric: Rubric for Executive Summary Peer Review

 

Please see below for submission instructions:

  • Students must submit their Executive Summary along with the completed rubric received from their peer reviewer.
  • Executive Summaries and Peer Review Rubrics must be uploaded separately.
  • Executive Summary Files must be uploaded as word documents. Peer Reviews may be uploaded as PDFs. 
  • Executive Summaries must use the following file name: Last Name, First Name Executive Summary
  • Peer Review Rubrics must use the following file name: Last Name, First Name Peer Review Rubrics
  • Students must submit their Executive Summary (Final) + Peer Review (of your Executive Summary Draft) documents by 11:59pm Wednesday, July 1.

items.title: APEx: Peer Review
items.details_html: Complete a structured peer review of a classmate's APEx Executive Summary. Reviewer assignments are made by program staff — check your Canvas inbox or the EXEC shell for your assigned peer.

items.title: Pharma S5 — Readings
items.details_html:
Required Readings

  • Weinmeyer — Direct-to-Consumer Advertising of Drugs — AMA Journal of Ethics, 2013. Examines the ethics and regulatory history of DTCA in the US, including the 1997 FDA guidance shift that opened TV advertising.
    ⏳ File not yet uploaded to Canvas. Check course Modules closer to the 2026-07-11 class date.
  • Kellogg case: Crestor (KEL245) — Kellogg School case KEL245. AstraZeneca's Crestor statin — case study in pharmaceutical marketing strategy, competitive positioning, and physician-directed vs. consumer-directed promotion.
    ⏳ File not yet uploaded to Canvas. Check course Modules closer to the 2026-07-11 class date.

Recommended Readings

  • Owermohle — Trump Crackdown on Pharma Advertising — Politico, Sep 2025. On proposed new federal restrictions on pharmaceutical advertising and the industry response.
    ⏳ File not yet uploaded to Canvas. Check course Modules closer to the 2026-07-11 class date.

readings_list.title: Owermohle — Trump Crackdown on Pharma Advertising

items.title: Pharma S6 — Readings
items.details_html:
Required Readings

  • Kesselheim, Avorn & Sarpatwari — The High Cost of Prescription Drugs in the US — JAMA, 2016. Comprehensive analysis of the structural and policy forces driving high drug prices in the US healthcare system. One of the most-cited papers in the field.
    ⏳ File not yet uploaded to Canvas. Check course Modules closer to the 2026-07-12 class date.
  • Brazil — How AI Is Transforming Drug Discovery — The Pharmaceutical Journal, 2024. Overview of current AI applications in drug discovery, target identification, and clinical trial design.
    ⏳ File not yet uploaded to Canvas. Check course Modules closer to the 2026-07-12 class date.
  • HBS case: Spark Therapeutics (818059) — Harvard Business School case 818059. Spark Therapeutics and the launch of Luxturna, the first FDA-approved gene therapy — examines pricing, value-based payment models, and stakeholder reactions.
    ⏳ File not yet uploaded to Canvas. Check course Modules closer to the 2026-07-12 class date.

Recommended Readings

  • Kenney & Gleason — 2024 AMCP Payment Model Survey — Journal of Managed Care & Specialty Pharmacy, 2025. Survey of evolving outcomes-based and value-based payment models for specialty and gene therapy drugs.
    ⏳ File not yet uploaded to Canvas. Check course Modules closer to the 2026-07-12 class date.

readings_list.title: Kesselheim, Avorn & Sarpatwari — The High Cost of Prescription Drugs in the US
Brazil — How AI Is Transforming Drug Discovery
Kenney & Gleason — 2024 AMCP Payment Model Survey

items.title: ILE Culminating Paper 2026
items.details_html:
Integrative Learning Experience (ILE)

The Council on Education for Public Health (CEPH) requires that all MPH students complete an Integrative Learning Experience (ILE).


In the Department of Health Policy & Management (HPM) at Columbia University Mailman School of Public Health, students complete a five-page culminating paper near the end of their program of study. The paper is an opportunity for students to demonstrate their ability to synthesize program competencies and apply them to address complex public health and health systems problems.

 

Students will choose one of two formats to fulfill the ILE requirement: (1) a policy proposal or (2) a business proposal. ILE papers are reviewed and approved by the student’s Faculty Advisor. The writing assignment is an opportunity for students to demonstrate their understanding of public health principles and practices, critical thinking skills, and the application of theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios.

  • This paper must be submitted to Canvas by August 9, 2026.
  • Your paper should have a minimum of five pages and a maximum 10 pages of prose.
  • Unlimited graphical exhibits are allowed and should be included at the end of the paper (after the endnotes and bibliography)
    1. All exhibits must be referenced, in numerical order, in the prose.
  • Papers should be double-spaced, 12-point font, one-inch margins.
  • Your paper will be graded by your Faculty Advisor.
  • A rubric has been uploaded to Canvas, against which you will be graded. You must Meet Expectations in all criteria to satisfy this requirement and pass. You will be notified in writing if your paper is awarded Distinction. Any paper that does not Meet Expectations will require resubmission that addresses all faculty comments. Papers should be resubmitted within 48 hours of notification.
  • Students may use AI tools to generate preliminary ideas and to conduct broad research around a topic. When doing so, it is mandatory for the student to declare the use of AI in their Works Cited or Bibliography. The verbatim use of AI-generated material or the use of AI-generated material with cursory semantic change is not permitted. You are responsible for all facts and citations within your work. The expectation is that the submitted work is original and is produced independently. You may not resubmit a paper that has been previously graded at Columbia University.
  • Please contact your Faculty Advisor or Casey Malone (cm3926@cumc.columbia.edu) with any questions.

 

Example papers for Business Plan: ILE EXEC Business Plan sample 1.pdf   ILE EXEC Business Plan Sample 2.pdf  ILE EXEC Business Plan Sample 3.pdf 

Example papers for Policy Proposal: ILE Paper Example 1.pdf  ILE Paper Example 4 (Policy).pdf

 

PAPER FORMATS

 

(1) Policy Proposal

 

The policy proposal should include the following elements:

  • Topic: Policy Question: What question will you examine in this proposal?
  • Executive Summary/Abstract (1/2 pg. max, 150 words)
  • What is the current problem the policy is aiming to address?
    • Historical background
    • Context
  • What are some possible solutions to this problem?
    • Pros and cons of each
  • Proposed Policy: What policy do you think is the best approach?
  • Evidence in Support
    • Why is this policy better than the alternatives?
    • Include an example to explain reasoning for why this is the best policy.
    • What data/evidence supports your proposal?
  • Politics of Policy
    • What is the political landscape surrounding this problem?
    • What social groups would be affected by this policy?
    • What other groups might get involved? (Non-profits? Political entities? Religious groups?)
    • Cost
    • Ideological arguments
    • Other considerations
  • Implementation
    • How will this be executed?

 

 

 

 

 

(2) Business Proposal (Strategic Plan, Program Plan, Project Design)

 

The Proposal should include the following elements:

  • Executive Summary (max one page)
    • What is the problem or opportunity you are trying to address?
    • Key innovative concept you are proposing
  • Environment/Market
    • Who are the key stakeholders?
    • What are the important needs/wants of these stakeholders, e.g. unmet user/patient needs, underserved markets, regulatory restrictions, intense competition (perhaps from a new market disruptor)?
    • How “big” is the market or population you intend to serve? Quantify the total need, the share of the population that is currently served in some way, and the un- or under-served segments you intend to focus on.
  • Given the items above, how will you address the need?
    • What options are available to you? (Discuss a minimum of two options)
    • What is your recommendation and why? (Based on what criteria?)
    • What metrics and targets will you measure to ensure success? (What can you measure to indicate progress or impact?)
    • What short-, medium-, and long-term implementation steps are required to achieve key targets?
    • What resources—budget, staffing, coordination, alliances, partnerships, etc.—are needed to ensure success?
    • What risks—and mitigating factors—need to be considered given your proposed course of action?

 

 

ILE Culminating Paper Rubric for EXEC Students

 

 

 

Requirement

Exceeds Mastery (3 points)

Meets Mastery

(2 points)

Near Mastery

(1 point)

Well Below Mastery

(0 points)

Writing skills: Organization

n/a

The paper follows the template, communicates ideas effectively, and presents ideas clearly.

n/a

Paper is disorganized and does not include all elements of the template.

Writing sills: Fundamentals

n/a

Polished grammar, syntax, and mechanics.

n/a

Poor grammar, syntax, and mechanics.

Writing skills: Tone

n/a

The paper has a professional tone appropriate to the expected audience. The case made is persuasive.

n/a

The tone is unprofessional or inappropriate to the expected audience. The case is not made or is not persuasive.

Interpret results of data analysis for public health research, policy, or practice.

Demonstrates a sophisticated interpretation of relevant data, including evidence that both supports and challenges the proposed recommendation. Data are analyzed (not merely described), clearly linked to the policy or strategic decision, and used persuasively to justify the chosen course of action.

The paper includes appropriate and accurate analysis of relevant data that is clearly connected to and supports the proposed policy initiative or business plan. Data interpretations are sound, though analysis may focus primarily on supporting evidence.

Data are included, but analysis is limited, descriptive, or superficial. Interpretations are weak or inconsistently connected to the policy proposal or strategic plan.

Data are absent, inaccurately interpreted, or not meaningfully connected to the policy proposal or strategic plan.

Propose strategies to identify stakeholders and build coalitions and partnerships for influencing public health outcomes.

Presents a comprehensive analysis of existing and potential stakeholders, including their interests, influence, and roles. It proposes a clear, innovative, and well-justified plan for engaging key partners and building coalitions that are likely to influence public health outcomes and advance the proposed initiative.

Includes a thoughtful and appropriate plan to identify key stakeholders and build coalitions or partnerships relevant to the proposed policy or strategic plan. Strategies are feasible and aligned with the intended public health outcomes.

Stakeholder or coalition strategies are included but lack sufficient detail, strategic rationale, or clear connection to the proposed initiative or public health outcomes.

Strategies for identifying stakeholders or building coalitions are absent, unclear, or inappropriate. Key populations, partners, or sectors are overlooked or inadequately addressed.

Apply fundamental principles of research methods and data management to develop and synthesize research questions in health policy and management.

Demonstrates rigorous and well-integrated application of appropriate research methods and data management principles. Data analysis is thorough, methodologically sound, and clearly aligned with the research question, persuasively demonstrating that the proposed policy or management approach is well founded.

Applies appropriate research methods and data management principles clearly and correctly to address the stated research question. Data analysis is coherent and supports the inquiry, though methodological justification or integration may be limited.

Application of research methods and data management principles is uneven, fragmented, or superficial. Data analysis is limited or inconsistently connected to the research question.

Research methods and data management principles are absent, unclear, or incorrectly applied. The research question is poorly defined or not meaningfully addressed through analysis.
